summary: Split the monolithic Crazy Hand translation unit into many per-motion ftCh_* units, converted Crazy Hand from one NonMatching object to address-ordered Matching objects in configure.py, and updated GALE01 splits/header includes accordingly. The automated decomp.dev report showed a large linking gain for GALE01: linked code +24,328 bytes, linked data +2,432 bytes, matched data +2,416 bytes, and 293 new matches; the many reported broken matches were mostly the old ftCh_Init unit losing symbols that reappeared under the new ftCrazyHand units.
what_changed: configure.py replaced `Object(NonMatching, "melee/ft/chara/ftCrazyHand/ftCh_Init.c")` with `Object(Matching, ...)` entries for ftCh_Init plus 34 additional Crazy Hand split files.

config/GALE01/splits.txt split the original ftCh_Init range `0x80155E18-0x8015BD20` into many address-contiguous units from ftCh_Init through ftCh_TagCancel, with per-unit `.text` and `.sdata2` ranges and `.data` ranges for Wait1_0 and GrabUnk1_B174.

ftCh_Init.c was reduced from the large all-in-one Crazy Hand implementation to the init/load/motion-state-table area; the action logic moved into new files such as ftCh_Wait1_0.c, ftCh_FingerBeam.c, ftCh_Poke.c, ftCh_Slam.c, ftCh_TagGrab.c, and capture/thrown state files.
ftCh_Init.h was stripped of most action prototypes; new per-file headers were added under ftCrazyHand and contain address-commented prototypes for each split unit.

Cross-unit include sites were fixed to include the specific new headers they need: examples include ftCo_Fall.c and ft_0892.c including ftCh_TagCancel.h, ft_0C8C.c including ftCh_TagGrab.h, fighter.c including ftCh_Wait1_0.h, ftmotionstates.c including Crazy Hand capture/thrown headers, and Master Hand tag/slam files including ftCh_Squeezing_1.h, ftCh_Throw.h, or ftCh_Squeeze.h.
smart_moves: The split followed the original address order exactly, so configure.py object order and GALE01 split ranges line up from `0x80155E18` through `0x8015BD20`; this is the right pattern for turning one nonmatching blob into linkable matching objects.
Large shared data was assigned to the units that own it after the split: Wait1_0 receives the `0x803D4878-0x803D4998` data range, while GrabUnk1_B174 receives `0x803D4998-0x803D49C0`.
The central ftCh_Init.h dependency surface was reduced and callers were migrated to precise headers, which makes future fighter work less likely to rely on a giant catch-all Crazy Hand header.
Uncertain or behavior-dependent symbols were not over-renamed; names like `fn_801582D8`, `ftCh_Init_801592D4`, and `ftCh_GrabUnk1_8015B174` preserve address identity until better names are justified.

Matching-sensitive code shape was preserved in the new translation units, including custom inline sqrt/frsqrte helpers and explicit padding/dummy locals such as `u32 pad`, `u8 _[...]`, and `PAD_STACK(8)`.
decomp_lessons: When a PR splits a monolithic translation unit, decomp.dev may show many broken matches in the old unit and matching gains in the new unit paths; compare the symbol names and linked-code totals before treating those as regressions.
For fighter boss splits, update three things together: configure.py object list, config/GALE01/splits.txt section ranges, and all headers/call sites that used prototypes from the old monolithic file.

Per-motion-state files with matching `.h` files are easier to maintain than a single large init header, especially when motion callbacks are referenced by motion-state tables and by other fighter modules.

Keep raw motion IDs and address-based names when the semantic name is not proven; this PR uses both named callbacks like `ftCh_BackAirplane2_Anim` and address names for unresolved init/helper functions.
Data and sdata2 placement matters as much as text placement when linking previously nonmatching fighter code; the split explicitly tracks `.data` and `.sdata2` ranges per new object.
naming_conventions: Crazy Hand split files use `ftCh_<Action>.c/.h`, with suffixes such as `_0`, `_1`, and numbered action phases for adjacent or related motion groups (`ftCh_FingerBeam_0`, `ftCh_RockCrush_0`, `ftCh_BackAirplane1`).
Motion callbacks follow the fighter convention `ftCh_<State>_Anim`, `ftCh_<State>_IASA`, `ftCh_<State>_Phys`, and `ftCh_<State>_Coll`.
Initializer/helper functions often retain address names under the `ftCh_Init_801xxxxx` or `ftCh_GrabUnk1_8015xxxx` pattern when the semantic purpose remains unclear.
Headers use address-based include guards such as `GALE01_1592D4` and address comments before prototypes, e.g. `/* 1592D4 */ void ftCh_Init_801592D4(HSD_GObj* gobj);`.
Common captured/thrown states keep `ftCo_...CrazyHand` callback names while living in ftCrazyHand split files, as shown by the new capture/damage/wait/thrown headers included from ftmotionstates.c.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Preserve object order in configure.py to match the GALE01 split order; the new Crazy Hand objects are listed in ascending address order from ftCh_Init to ftCh_TagCancel.

Move `.data` ownership with the code that references it rather than leaving all data on the old init unit; this PR relocates the Wait1_0 decision table and the GrabUnk1_B174 table into their new object ranges.
Keep local inline math helpers when they are codegen-sensitive: the split carries forward `my_sqrtf`/`my_lbVector_Len` using `__frsqrte` instead of substituting a library call.
Do not remove apparent dummy stack variables or padding during a split; the new files retain `u32 pad`, `u8 _[...]`, and `PAD_STACK(8)` patterns seen in the diff.
Use exact include dependencies after splitting so motion-state tables and cross-fighter interactions resolve without relying on ftCh_Init.h as a global prototype bucket.
review_feedback: No PR body explanation was provided in the slice.
No inline review comments were present.
jurrejelle submitted an APPROVED review with an empty body shortly before merge.

The automated decomp-dev comment reported GALE01 progress: matched code 67.77% (+0.03%, +1172 bytes), linked code 39.86% (+0.63%, +24328 bytes), matched data 39.65% (+0.20%, +2416 bytes), and linked data 33.46% (+0.20%, +2432 bytes).
follow_up_queries: Search `src/melee/ft/chara/ftCrazyHand` for remaining `fn_8015` and `ftCh_Init_8015` names that can be semantically renamed after behavior is verified.
Audit raw Crazy Hand motion IDs such as `0x16F`, `0x170`, and nearby values in the new files and replace with enum names only where the mapping is proven.
Check whether the ftCo_* CrazyHand capture/thrown callbacks need clearer ownership or documentation now that they live in separate ftCrazyHand headers.
Review placeholder/platform includes and matching-only padding in the new split files before cleanup; changes may affect matching.
Use the decomp.dev new-match/broken-match pairs to confirm that the ftCh_Init split did not introduce a real regression beyond unit relocation.
